meta.title: AI Policy Pyramid
meta.subtitle: Build your AI governance hierarchy across the CoSAI AI SRF five layers
sources: CoSAI AI Shared Responsibility Framework, V0.7 (2026)

copy.intro: Your AI governance hierarchy in one place — principles, policies, standards, guidelines, and baselines, each tagged to the CoSAI AI SRF layer that owns it. Adopt entries from the library, add your own, and trace the cascade from values down to evidence.
copy.privacy: Everything stays in your browser — no backend, no telemetry. Your pyramid is persisted in localStorage and you can export/import it as JSON.
copy.emptyState: Your pyramid is empty. Adopt entries from the library or add a custom one to get started.

levels.label: Principles
levels.description: Core values and ethical commitments that guide every AI decision
levels.guidance: What we believe. Principles are durable, aspirational, and rarely change. They give policies their authority.

levels.label: Policies
levels.description: Mandatory rules derived from regulation and senior leadership direction
levels.guidance: What we will do. Policies translate principles and external obligations into binding organisational rules.

levels.label: Standards
levels.description: Specific, measurable requirements driven by customer and market expectations
levels.guidance: How we will do it. Standards are the testable, auditable shape of a policy — concrete enough to build evidence against.

levels.label: Guidelines
levels.description: Recommended best practices for teams — advisory, not mandatory
levels.guidance: How teams should do it. Guidelines codify good practice without binding force; they are the preferred way to meet a standard.

levels.label: Baselines
levels.description: Minimum evidence requirements and technical controls
levels.guidance: What evidence proves it. Baselines are the floor of acceptable technical implementation — the artefacts an auditor can look at.

templates.title: Human Dignity & Rights
templates.statement: We are committed to developing and deploying AI that respects human dignity, autonomy, and fundamental rights. No AI system will be used in ways that demean, manipulate, or deprive individuals of their rights.
templates.driverSource: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021)

templates.title: Transparency & Explainability
templates.statement: Our AI systems will be transparent about their capabilities and limitations and subject to meaningful human oversight. We will provide explanations of AI-driven decisions that affect individuals to the extent technically feasible.
templates.driverSource: NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.1; EU AI Act Recital 47

templates.title: Fairness & Non-Discrimination
templates.statement: We will proactively identify, measure, and mitigate bias in our AI systems. AI must not create or reinforce unlawful discrimination on the basis of protected characteristics.
templates.driverSource: IEEE Ethically Aligned Design; NIST AI 100-1 §4.3

templates.title: Accountability
templates.statement: Clear human accountability for AI decisions is assigned, documented, and enforced. Every AI system has a designated owner responsible for its governance, performance, and risk.
templates.driverSource: NIST AI RMF GOVERN 2.1; ISO 42001 Clause 5.3

templates.title: Safety First
templates.statement: Safety is a prerequisite for deployment. AI systems that cannot be made safe — through technical controls, human oversight, or operational constraints — will not be deployed in production.
templates.driverSource: NIST AI 100-1 §4.2; EU AI Act Art. 9

templates.title: Privacy by Design
templates.statement: Privacy is embedded into AI system design from the outset, not bolted on. We minimize data collection, protect personal information, and give individuals meaningful control over their data.

templates.title: Robustness & Security
templates.statement: Our AI systems must be technically robust and secure throughout their lifecycle. Systems shall function reliably under normal use, foreseeable misuse, and adversarial conditions. We implement cybersecurity controls, continuous monitoring, and fallback mechanisms.
templates.driverSource: OECD AI Principle 1.4; EU AI Act Art. 15; ISO 42001 A.6.2

templates.title: Sustainability & Inclusive Benefit
templates.statement: We measure and minimize the environmental impact of our AI systems and design for inclusive growth. Sustainability and well-being are factors in AI procurement and design decisions.
templates.driverSource: EU AI Act Art. 68; OECD AI Principle 1.1

templates.title: EU AI Act Provider Obligations
templates.statement: As a provider of AI systems placed on the EU market, we comply with all obligations under EU AI Act Articles 16–27: conformity assessment, technical documentation, quality management, post-market monitoring, and incident reporting for high-risk AI systems.

templates.title: EU AI Act Deployer Obligations
templates.statement: As a deployer of high-risk AI systems we comply with EU AI Act Article 26 obligations: maintaining human oversight, using systems only for intended purposes, ensuring staff competency, and logging operations as required.

templates.title: High-Risk AI System Controls
templates.statement: AI systems classified as high-risk under EU AI Act Annex III require: risk management systems, data governance, technical documentation, transparency to users, human oversight measures, accuracy standards, and cybersecurity controls.
templates.driverSource: EU AI Act Annex III; Art. 9-15

templates.title: AI Risk Governance Policy
templates.statement: We implement an AI risk governance framework aligned with NIST AI RMF GOVERN function. This includes defining AI risk tolerance, establishing roles and responsibilities, creating policies and procedures, and ensuring organizational accountability for AI risk management.

templates.title: AI & HIPAA Compliance
templates.statement: AI systems that access, process, or generate Protected Health Information (PHI) must comply with HIPAA Security Rule §164.308: access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, and transmission security. All AI vendors with PHI access require a Business Associate Agreement (BAA).

templates.title: AI & Fair Credit Reporting
templates.statement: AI systems used in credit decisioning must comply with the Fair Credit Reporting Act. Adverse action notices must be provided when AI contributes to credit denials. AI models must not use prohibited data as proxy variables for credit decisions.

templates.title: Model Risk Management (SR 11-7)
templates.statement: AI/ML models used in financial decision-making must follow model risk management practices per SR 11-7 or equivalent: model validation, documentation, ongoing monitoring, and board-level oversight of material model risk.

templates.title: AI & GDPR Obligations
templates.statement: AI systems processing personal data of EU/UK residents must comply with GDPR. Article 22 restrictions on solely automated decision-making apply. DPIAs are required for high-risk AI processing. Data subjects retain rights to explanation and human review.

templates.title: ISO 42001 AI Management System Policy
templates.statement: The organization commits to establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an AI Management System (AIMS) aligned with ISO/IEC 42001. Top management establishes an AI policy appropriate to the organization's purpose and supporting strategic direction.

templates.title: Federal AI Use (EO 14110 / OMB M-24-10)
templates.statement: Federal contractors using AI in scope of EO 14110 must comply with safety evaluations, transparency reports, and red-team testing for dual-use foundation models. Public-sector AI use must follow OMB M-24-10 minimum risk-management practices.

templates.title: SOC 2 Type II for AI Systems
templates.statement: AI systems and supporting infrastructure must satisfy SOC 2 Type II Trust Services Criteria, with emphasis on CC6 (Logical and Physical Access Controls) and CC7 (System Operations). Annual Type II audits with unqualified opinions are required for customer-facing AI services.

templates.title: ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS Coverage
templates.statement: AI systems and associated data assets are in scope of the ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Information Security Management System. Annex A controls relevant to AI (A.8 Technology, A.5 Organizational) are implemented and audited annually.

templates.title: Model Cards Documentation
templates.statement: Every AI model deployed in production must have a published model card documenting: intended use, out-of-scope uses, training data summary, evaluation results by demographic group, ethical considerations, and known limitations. Model cards are updated at each major version.
templates.driverSource: Mitchell et al. (2019); NIST AI RMF MAP 2.2

templates.title: EU AI Act Conformity Assessment
templates.statement: High-risk AI systems require a conformity assessment before market placement. Assessment must verify compliance with EU AI Act Art. 9–15 and be documented in a Declaration of Conformity. Third-party assessment is required for biometric and critical-infrastructure AI.

templates.title: AI Bill of Materials (AIBOM)
templates.statement: All AI systems must maintain an AI Bill of Materials documenting: base models, fine-tuning datasets, third-party components, training libraries, and inference dependencies. AIBOMs follow SPDX or CycloneDX-ML format and are updated at each release.
templates.driverSource: NTIA SBOM Minimum Elements; CycloneDX ML-BOM

templates.title: Bias & Fairness Testing Standard
templates.statement: Before production deployment, AI systems used in consequential decisions must pass bias and fairness testing across protected characteristics, using demographically representative evaluation sets. Results are documented and material disparities addressed before launch.

templates.title: Explainability Documentation Standard
templates.statement: AI systems used for consequential individual decisions must provide post-hoc explanations using approved methods (SHAP, LIME, or equivalent). Explanations must be validated for faithfulness and completeness, documented in technical specifications.
templates.driverSource: NIST AI RMF MAP 2.2; EU AI Act Art. 13

templates.title: AI Incident Response Standard
templates.statement: A documented AI incident response plan must be maintained, tested annually, and activated within one hour of confirmed AI system failure or misuse. Serious incidents affecting EU citizens are reported to national authorities within 15 days per EU AI Act Art. 73.
templates.driverSource: NIST SP 800-61r3; EU AI Act Art. 73

templates.title: Training Data Quality Standard
templates.statement: Training and fine-tuning datasets must meet documented quality criteria: completeness, accuracy, representativeness, and currency. Data lineage must be traceable from source to model. PII in training data requires privacy review and lawful-basis documentation.

templates.title: Red Team Testing Standard
templates.statement: High-risk and customer-facing AI systems must undergo red-team adversarial testing before production deployment and annually thereafter. Testing covers prompt injection, jailbreaks, data extraction, and misuse scenarios using MITRE ATLAS or equivalent methodology.

templates.title: Document AI System Limitations
templates.statement: Document known limitations, failure modes, and out-of-distribution behaviors in model cards and system documentation before each deployment. Limitations should be communicated to end users in plain language.

templates.title: Pre-Deployment Bias Reviews
templates.statement: Conduct a structured bias review before each production deployment of AI systems that affect individuals. Include diverse stakeholders in the review. Document findings and remediation actions.

templates.title: Involve Domain Experts in Data Review
templates.statement: Involve domain experts (clinical, legal, financial as appropriate) in AI training-data review and evaluation design. Expert review catches domain-specific biases and quality issues that automated checks miss.

templates.title: Quarterly AI Ethics Reviews
templates.statement: Perform quarterly AI ethics reviews with cross-functional teams (product, legal, engineering, affected business units). Review covers new AI use cases, incidents, regulatory developments, and emerging risks.

templates.title: Explainability Tools for High-Stakes Decisions
templates.statement: Use explainability tools (SHAP, LIME, attention visualization, or equivalent) for AI systems making high-stakes decisions. Make explanations available to decision-makers and, where legally required, to affected individuals.

templates.title: Catalog Shadow AI Before Integration
templates.statement: Identify and assess all shadow AI (AI tools used without formal approval) before integrating into business processes. Uncatalogued AI use creates unmanaged risk and compliance exposure.

templates.title: Monitor Model Drift
templates.statement: Monitor AI model performance and data drift in production. Define drift thresholds and retraining schedules based on system risk level. Document and review drift events.

templates.title: Disclose AI Use to Affected Individuals
templates.statement: Notify users when they are interacting with an AI system or when an AI system has made or significantly influenced a decision affecting them. Disclosure should be timely, clear, and accessible.
templates.driverSource: EU AI Act Art. 50; NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.7

templates.title: MFA for All AI System Access
templates.statement: Multi-factor authentication is required for all human access to AI systems, training infrastructure, model registries, and production APIs. Exceptions require a documented waiver approved by CISO or equivalent.

templates.title: Logging & Monitoring on Production AI
templates.statement: Logging and monitoring must be active on all production AI systems. Logs must capture inputs/outputs (where privacy permits), inference latency, error rates, and access events. Logs are retained ≥ 12 months and reviewed for anomalies.
templates.driverSource: NIST SP 800-92; EU AI Act Art. 12

templates.title: AI Incident Response Plan
templates.statement: A documented AI incident response plan must be maintained and tested at least annually via tabletop exercise. The plan covers detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident review.

templates.title: Vendor Security Attestation on File
templates.statement: For each AI vendor or cloud provider with access to organizational data or systems, a current SOC 2 Type II report (or equivalent attestation) must be on file. Attestations are reviewed annually and before contract renewal.
templates.driverSource: SOC 2; ISO 27001; NIST AI RMF GOVERN 6.2

templates.title: Agentic Guardrails & Human-in-the-Loop
templates.statement: Autonomous and semi-autonomous AI agents must have documented guardrails limiting scope of action and defined human-in-the-loop checkpoints for irreversible or high-impact decisions. Guardrail configurations are version-controlled and change-managed.
templates.driverSource: NIST AI 100-1 §4.2; CoSAI Agentic AI SRF

templates.title: Data Classification Tags on Training Inputs
templates.statement: All datasets used for training, fine-tuning, or RAG must carry classification tags (public / internal / confidential / restricted) and a source-provenance record. Restricted data may not be used in models with broader access.
